teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** The partial defence of loss of control, enacted under ss.54-55 of the Coroners and Justice Act 2009, replaced the old common law defence of provocation (which had been given statutory form in s.3 of the Homicide Act 1957) on 4 October 2010. It reduces murder to voluntary manslaughter where D kills as a result of a loss of self-control caused by a qualifying trigger.

**Old Law — Provocation (s.3 Homicide Act 1957):** The old defence required D to have been provoked (by things done or said) to lose self-control, and the jury had to consider whether a reasonable person would have done as D did. The law was widely criticised as anachronistic, sexist (favouring male responses to infidelity), and excessively complex. The 'reasonable person' test in *R v Smith (Morgan)* [2001] 1 AC 146 (allowing D's depression to be considered on the objective test) was reversed by *Attorney General for Jersey v Holley* [2005] UKPC 23, highlighting the doctrinal confusion.

**New Law — Loss of Control (ss.54-55 CJA 2009):** The new defence has three stages: (1) D must have lost self-control (subjective — s.54(1)); (2) the loss of self-control must be attributable to a qualifying trigger (s.55); and (3) a person of D's sex and age, with a normal degree of tolerance and self-restraint, might have reacted in the same or a similar way (objective — s.54(1)(c)). Sexual infidelity is expressly excluded as a qualifying trigger (s.55(6)(c)), BUT in *R v Clinton* [2012] EWCA Crim 2, the Court of Appeal held that sexual infidelity, while not a trigger on its own, may form part of the context in which other qualifying triggers operate. It is not wholly irrelevant.

**In short:** The new law is narrower and more structured. The three-stage test (loss of control, qualifying trigger, objective standard) replaced the loose provocation framework. *Clinton* [2012] ensures sexual infidelity is not entirely excluded but cannot standing alone constitute a trigger.
oscoaReferences: Attorney General for Jersey v Holley [2005] UKPC 23

question: The partial defence of loss of control, which reduces murder to voluntary manslaughter, is governed by sections 54 and __________ of the Coroners and Justice Act 2009, replacing the old provocation defence under s.3 of the Homicide Act 1957.
feedback: Correct. Sections 54-55 CJA 2009 constitute the new statutory framework. S.54 sets out the three-stage test; s.55 defines the qualifying triggers. The old s.3 Homicide Act 1957 was repealed.

question: R v __________ [2012] EWCA Crim 2 held that while sexual infidelity is expressly excluded as a qualifying trigger under s.55(6)(c) CJA 2009, it may form part of the contextual background to other qualifying triggers.
feedback: Correct. *R v Clinton* [2012] — D killed his wife after discovering her sexual infidelity and enduring taunts about his suicidal thoughts. The Court of Appeal held that sexual infidelity, while excluded as a standalone trigger, is not so excluded that it must be entirely erased from the narrative. It may contextualise other triggers — here, the taunts about suicide constituted a qualifying trigger under s.55(4).

question: Arrange the three stages of the loss of control defence under s.54 CJA 2009 in the correct order:
answer: Stage 1: D's acts resulted from a loss of self-control → Stage 2: The loss of self-control was attributable to a qualifying trigger → Stage 3: A person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance and self-restraint might have reacted similarly

options.text: Stage 1 (Subjective): D's acts in killing resulted from a loss of self-control (s.54(1)(a))
Stage 2 (Qualifying Trigger): The loss of self-control was attributable to a qualifying trigger (s.55)
Stage 3 (Objective): A person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance and self-restraint might have reacted the same or similarly (s.54(1)(c))

feedback: Correct. The three-stage test is sequential. If Stage 1 fails (no loss of self-control), the defence fails at the threshold. Stage 2 identifies whether the trigger meets the statutory definition. Stage 3 applies the normative, objective filter. All three must be satisfied.

question: In 2008, George discovered his wife was having an affair. He went to her lover's house, confronted him, and killed him in a violent rage. He now seeks to rely on provocation. Applying the law before the CJA 2009:

options.text: George cannot rely on provocation because sexual infidelity was excluded even under the old law
options.feedback: Incorrect. The old law (s.3 Homicide Act 1957) did not expressly exclude sexual infidelity. It was a jury question whether the provocation was sufficient.

options.text: The old provocation defence under s.3 Homicide Act 1957 would apply — the killing occurred before 4 October 2010, so the old law governs
options.feedback: Correct. The CJA 2009 provisions came into force on 4 October 2010. Killings before that date are governed by the old provocation law under s.3 Homicide Act 1957. Under the old law, sexual infidelity could constitute provocation, and the jury would consider the reasonable person test (as modified by *Holley* [2005]).

options.text: George cannot rely on any partial defence because the CJA 2009 abolished provocation entirely
options.feedback: Incorrect. The CJA 2009 replaced provocation with loss of control — it did not abolish the partial defence entirely. It also applies prospectively only.

options.text: Both the old and new law would produce the same result — the date of the killing is irrelevant
options.feedback: Incorrect. The transitional provisions of the CJA 2009 mean the old law applies to killings before October 2010 and the new law applies thereafter. The outcomes may differ, particularly regarding sexual infidelity.

feedback: The transitional provision is important — the CJA 2009 provisions are not retrospective. Killings before 4 October 2010 are governed by the old provocation law. Under the old s.3 Homicide Act 1957, sexual infidelity could constitute provocation; under s.55(6)(c) CJA 2009, it is expressly excluded as a standalone trigger.

question:
Spot the legal error in this statement:

'The new law of loss of control under the CJA 2009 is the same as the old provocation defence — it just has a different name. A defendant can rely on sexual infidelity as a qualifying trigger, just as they could under the old law.'
answer: Two errors. (1) The new law is NOT the same — it has a three-stage structure, narrower qualifying triggers, and a more restrictive objective test. (2) Sexual infidelity is expressly excluded as a qualifying trigger under s.55(6)(c) CJA 2009, whereas the old law allowed it. Sexual infidelity can only be contextual background (*Clinton* [2012]), not a standalone trigger.
feedback: Found them. The CJA 2009 was specifically designed to reform provocation, making it narrower and more principled. The express exclusion of sexual infidelity is one of the most significant changes. *Clinton* [2012] confirms only a residual contextual role for sexual infidelity — it cannot be the sole qualifying trigger.

question: Which TWO of the following are key changes introduced by the CJA 2009 loss of control defence compared to the old provocation defence?

options.text: The reasonable person test was entirely removed from the law
options.feedback: Incorrect. The objective test remains but in a modified form — a person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance and self-restraint (s.54(1)(c)).

options.text: Sexual infidelity is expressly excluded as a qualifying trigger
options.feedback: Correct. Section 55(6)(c) CJA 2009 expressly excludes sexual infidelity as a qualifying trigger. This is one of the most significant changes from the old law. However, *Clinton* [2012] confirmed it may provide relevant context.

options.text: The partial defence is now available for attempted murder
options.feedback: Incorrect. Loss of control is a partial defence to murder only; it reduces murder to voluntary manslaughter. There is no partial defence for attempted murder.

options.text: The defence requires a specific qualifying trigger defined by statute, rather than the broad common law concept of provocation
options.feedback: Correct. Section 55 defines two specific qualifying triggers: fear of serious violence and things done or said of an extremely grave character causing a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged. This is narrower than the old broad provocation concept.

options.text: The defence no longer requires D to have lost self-control
options.feedback: Incorrect. Loss of self-control remains the first stage of the defence — s.54(1)(a). However, the requirement has been refined: it need not be 'sudden' (s.54(2)), allowing for 'slow-burn' reactions, but loss of control remains essential.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). The CJA 2009 introduced a structured three-stage test with defined qualifying triggers and expressly excluded sexual infidelity. The objective test remains but is modified, and loss of self-control is still required, though 'suddenness' is no longer a strict requirement.

question: Under s.54(2) of the CJA 2009, the loss of self-control need not be __________ — the old common law requirement of a sudden and temporary loss of self-control has been abolished.
feedback: Correct. S.54(2) CJA 2009 expressly provides that loss of self-control need not be sudden. This addresses the criticism that the old law discriminated against women and victims of domestic abuse who may experience a 'slow-burn' reaction rather than an immediate outburst. However, a delay may make it harder to satisfy the loss of control requirement.

question: Fatima discovers her husband has been having an affair for two years. She confronts him; he admits it and says he is leaving her for the other woman. Fatima, in distress, picks up a kitchen knife and kills him. She raises the defence of loss of control. The killing occurred in 2015. Applying *Clinton* [2012]:

options.text: The defence must fail — sexual infidelity is expressly excluded, and this case involves nothing but sexual infidelity
options.feedback: Incorrect. While sexual infidelity alone is excluded, the disclosure of infidelity combined with other conduct may engage different qualifying triggers.

options.text: The defence succeeds because sexual infidelity always qualifies under the old law
options.feedback: Incorrect. The killing occurred in 2015, so the CJA 2009 applies. Sexual infidelity is expressly excluded as a standalone trigger under s.55(6)(c).

options.text: The sexual infidelity is excluded as a standalone trigger, but if the husband's words (admission and declaration of leaving) were of an 'extremely grave character' causing a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged, they may constitute a separate qualifying trigger under s.55(4)
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Clinton* [2012], sexual infidelity cannot itself be the trigger. But the husband's words — his admission and announcement — may independently qualify if they are of an extremely grave character causing a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged (s.55(4)). The sexual context provides relevant background but is not the trigger itself.

options.text: Fatima has no partial defence because loss of control is not available for marital killings
options.feedback: Incorrect. Loss of control is available as a partial defence regardless of the relationship between D and V, provided the statutory conditions are met.

feedback: The *Clinton* [2012] distinction is subtle but crucial. Where sexual infidelity is intertwined with other conduct — particularly words that are taunting, humiliating, or cruel — the non-infidelity elements may constitute a qualifying trigger under s.55(4). The court must distinguish the sexual infidelity (excluded) from the words/conduct (potentially qualifying).
oscoaReferences: Coroners and Justice Act 2009, s.55(4), s.55(6)(c)

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Section 55 of the CJA 2009 defines two qualifying triggers for the loss of control defence. A qualifying trigger must have caused D's loss of self-control. The two triggers are: (1) fear of serious violence from V against D or another identified person (the 'fear trigger' — s.55(3)), and (2) things done or said (or both) which constituted circumstances of an extremely grave character and caused D to have a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged (the 'anger trigger' — s.55(4)). A combination of both may apply (s.55(5)).

**Fear of Serious Violence (*R v Dawes* [2013] EWCA Crim 322):** D and his co-accused discovered V — D's estranged wife's lover — asleep on a sofa. D attacked V and killed him with a bottle. The Court of Appeal emphasised that the fear trigger under s.55(3) must be genuine fear of serious violence at the time of the killing. There was no evidence of such fear — D was the aggressor. The court also held that D cannot rely on the fear trigger if he has incited the violence or is the aggressor (s.55(6)(a)).

**Anger Trigger — Extremely Grave Character (*R v Bowyer* [2013] EWCA Crim 322):** D, a burglar, killed the householder who disturbed him. He claimed the victim had taunted him about his background. The Court of Appeal held that the circumstances must be of an extremely grave character objectively — the test is not what D subjectively thought. A burglar confronted by a householder is not 'seriously wronged.' The s.55(4) trigger requires D to have been genuinely wronged in a way that would cause a justifiable sense of grievance in a person of normal tolerance.

**In short:** The fear trigger requires genuine fear of serious violence (not anger or aggression). The anger trigger requires circumstances of extremely grave character causing a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged — a high threshold that excludes trivial provocations, ordinary arguments, and self-induced situations.

question: Under s.55(3) of the CJA 2009, a qualifying trigger exists if D's loss of self-control was attributable to D's __________ of serious violence from V against D or another identified person.
feedback: Correct. Section 55(3) — the 'fear trigger.' D must genuinely fear serious violence from V. This trigger often arises in the context of domestic abuse where the victim lashes out against a long-term abuser.

question: R v __________ [2013] EWCA Crim 322 held that D cannot rely on the fear trigger under s.55(3) where D is the aggressor or has incited the violence — the fear of violence must be genuine, not self-induced.
feedback: Correct. *R v Dawes* [2013] — D attacked the man asleep on his estranged wife's sofa. The Court of Appeal held there was no evidence of fear of serious violence; D was the aggressor. Section 55(6)(a) also excludes the fear trigger where D incited the violence to provide an excuse for violence.

question: R v __________ [2013] EWCA Crim 322 held that for the anger trigger under s.55(4), the things done or said must be of an extremely grave character and cause a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged — a burglar is not 'seriously wronged' by being confronted by the householder.
feedback: Correct. *R v Bowyer* [2013] — D, a burglar, killed the householder who disturbed him. The Court of Appeal held the s.55(4) threshold was not met — the circumstances were not of an extremely grave character, and D had no justifiable sense of being seriously wronged. The test is objective.

question: Match each qualifying trigger to its statutory provision and key authority:
answer: Fear of serious violence=s.55(3) CJA 2009 — *R v Dawes* [2013]; Things done/said of extremely grave character=s.55(4) CJA 2009 — *R v Bowyer* [2013]; Combination of both triggers=s.55(5) CJA 2009; D incited the violence=s.55(6)(a) — trigger excluded

options.pair: Things done/said of extremely grave character

options.text: s.55(6)(a) — trigger excluded

feedback: Well matched. The two triggers operate independently or in combination. The exclusion provisions under s.55(6) are equally important — they restrict the defence where D incited the violence or seeks to rely on sexual infidelity alone.

question: Liam has been subjected to months of serious physical abuse by his partner, Victor. One evening, Victor raises his fist and says, 'Tonight, I'm going to finish you off.' Liam, genuinely terrified, grabs a nearby heavy ornament and strikes Victor, killing him instantly. Victor was unarmed at that moment. Applying the fear trigger under s.55(3):

options.text: The fear trigger cannot apply because Victor was unarmed at the precise moment of the killing
options.feedback: Incorrect. The fear trigger does not require V to be armed at the moment of the killing. The court considers the cumulative history of violence and whether D genuinely feared serious violence.

options.text: The fear trigger may apply — Liam's fear of serious violence, assessed in the context of the history of abuse and Victor's immediate threat, could constitute a qualifying trigger under s.55(3)
options.feedback: Correct. Under s.55(3), the question is whether D feared serious violence, judged against the full factual background. Liam's history of abuse gives Victor's threat its character. The threat 'I'm going to finish you off' and the raised fist, in that context, support a genuine fear of serious violence.

options.text: The fear trigger cannot apply because the defence requires D to have waited for V to strike first
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no requirement that V strike first. The defence is available for preventive action taken in fear of serious violence. However, the force must not be disproportionate — this is assessed at the third stage.

options.text: Liam cannot rely on the defence because he used a weapon — loss of control is not available to defendants who use weapons
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no categorical exclusion for defendants who use weapons. The use of a weapon goes to proportionality at Stage 3, not to the qualification of the trigger at Stage 2.

feedback: The fear trigger is particularly important for victims of domestic abuse who kill their abusers. The courts assess the fear in the context of the history of the relationship. The immediacy of the threat, combined with a documented history of violence, strongly supports a genuine fear of serious violence. The defence is available even where D strikes first, provided the fear was genuine and the response was not disproportionate.

question:
Spot the legal error in this jury direction:

'You may find a qualifying trigger under s.55(4) whenever D was seriously annoyed by the victim's words. The test is whether D himself felt seriously wronged. It does not matter whether a reasonable person would consider the circumstances grave — what matters is D's subjective reaction.'
answer: The s.55(4) trigger requires an objective assessment: the circumstances must be of an extremely grave character AND cause a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged. It is not enough that D was 'annoyed' or 'subjectively felt wronged' — the test is objective (*Bowyer* [2013]). Trivial provocations do not qualify.
feedback: Found it. The direction incorrectly substitutes an objective test with a subjective one. *R v Bowyer* [2013] — D, a burglar, claimed taunts triggered him. The Court of Appeal held the circumstances must objectively be of an extremely grave character. A burglar cannot have a 'justifiable sense' of being wronged by being confronted. The s.55(4) threshold is deliberately high.

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the qualifying triggers under s.55 CJA 2009?

options.text: The fear trigger under s.55(3) requires D to have genuinely feared serious violence from V against D or another identified person
options.feedback: Correct. Section 55(3) — the fear must be of serious violence, not merely minor assault, and must be directed at D or an identified other person.

options.text: Any insult, however minor, can constitute a qualifying trigger under s.55(4) if D subjectively found it upsetting
options.feedback: Incorrect. The s.55(4) trigger requires circumstances of an extremely grave character causing a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged. This is an objective test (*Bowyer* [2013]). Minor insults do not qualify.

options.text: A defendant who has incited violence as an excuse to use violence cannot rely on the fear trigger under s.55(6)(a)
options.feedback: Correct. Section 55(6)(a) expressly excludes the fear trigger where D incited the violence to provide an excuse for using force. *Dawes* [2013] — D was the aggressor and could not then claim fear.

options.text: The two triggers are mutually exclusive — D must choose either the fear trigger or the anger trigger but not both
options.feedback: Incorrect. Section 55(5) expressly provides that the triggers may operate in combination. For example, a victim of domestic abuse may both fear violence and feel seriously wronged by the conduct.

options.text: The s.55(4) trigger can be based purely on D's subjective feelings of grievance
options.feedback: Incorrect. The s.55(4) trigger is objective — the circumstances must be of an extremely grave character and must cause a justifiable (objectively assessed) sense of being seriously wronged.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (c). The fear trigger requires genuine fear of serious violence, and it is excluded where D incited the violence. The anger trigger has a high objective threshold. Both triggers may operate in combination under s.55(5).

question: Under s.55(4), the things done or said must constitute circumstances of an __________ grave character and must have caused D to have a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged.
feedback: Correct. The s.55(4) threshold is 'extremely grave character.' This deliberately sets a high bar — ordinary quarrels, insults, and minor grievances do not qualify. The sense of being wronged must be 'justifiable' — an objective standard.

question: Brendan, a burglar, breaks into a house at night. The householder wakes up and confronts him, shouting, 'Get out of my house, you scumbag!' Brendan claims this insult caused him to lose control and he beat the householder to death. Applying *Bowyer* [2013] and s.55(4):

options.text: Brendan has a qualifying trigger — the householder's words were insulting, and Brendan subjectively felt wronged
options.feedback: Incorrect. The s.55(4) test is objective. A burglar confronted by the householder cannot have a 'justifiable' sense of being seriously wronged.

options.text: The anger trigger applies because 'scumbag' is an insult of extremely grave character
options.feedback: Incorrect. Being called a 'scumbag' is not a circumstance of an extremely grave character. The threshold is high — serious taunting or sustained humiliation is required, not a single insult.

options.text: No qualifying trigger — Brendan was a burglar committing a crime, and a householder's right to protest does not engage s.55(4). The circumstances are not of an extremely grave character, and Brendan has no justifiable sense of being seriously wronged
options.feedback: Correct. This mirrors *R v Bowyer* [2013] directly. The court held that a burglar confronted by a householder cannot satisfy the s.55(4) trigger. The circumstances are not of an extremely grave character, and there is no justifiable grievance — the householder was lawfully protecting their home.

options.text: The fear trigger applies because Brendan feared violence from the householder
options.feedback: Incorrect. Brendan was the aggressor and intruder. He cannot claim fear of violence in circumstances he created (s.55(6)(a)).

feedback: This directly echoes *R v Bowyer* [2013] EWCA Crim 322. The s.55(4) threshold is high and objective. A person committing a crime cannot transform lawful confrontation by the victim into a qualifying trigger. The phrase 'extremely grave character' and 'justifiable sense of being seriously wronged' establish a normative, not subjective, test.

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** The third stage of the loss of control defence is the objective test under s.54(1)(c): would a person of D's sex and age, with a normal degree of tolerance and self-restraint and in the circumstances of D, have reacted in the same or a similar way? This is a normative filter — even if D lost self-control and had a qualifying trigger, the defence fails if a person of normal tolerance and self-restraint would not have killed.

**The Standard — 'Normal Tolerance and Self-Restraint':** The test excludes circumstances whose only relevance is that they bear on D's general capacity for tolerance and self-restraint (s.54(3)). In other words, D's personal characteristics that merely make them more irritable or susceptible to losing control are excluded — the standard is a person of normal tolerance. But D's sex and age *are* considered.

***R v Asmelash* [2013] EWCA Crim 157:** D killed V in a drunken fight. The Court of Appeal held that voluntary intoxication is wholly irrelevant to the objective test. D's drunkenness is not a 'circumstance of D' for the purposes of s.54(1)(c) — it is a factor bearing on D's reduced tolerance and self-restraint, which is expressly excluded by s.54(3). The objective standard remains that of a sober person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance.

***R v Rejmanski* [2017] EWCA Crim 2061:** D, a Polish national and former soldier suffering from PTSD and adjustment disorder, killed V. The Court held that mental disorders are excluded from the 'circumstances of D' on the objective test to the extent they merely reduce D's tolerance or self-restraint. However, any qualifying trigger must also be judged from D's perspective as shaped by the circumstances — this relates to the gravity of the trigger (Stage 2), not to D's capacity for self-control (Stage 3). The objective test is uniform: a person of normal tolerance and self-restraint.

**In short:** The objective test asks whether a person of the same sex and age, with normal tolerance and self-restraint, might have killed. Personal characteristics that diminish self-control (drunkenness, short temper, mental disorders reducing restraint) are excluded. The standard is normative and demanding.
oscoaReferences: Coroners and Justice Act 2009, s.54(1)(c), s.54(3)

question: Under s.54(1)(c) CJA 2009, the objective test asks whether a person of D's sex and age, with a __________ degree of tolerance and self-restraint and in the circumstances of D, might have reacted in the same or a similar way.
feedback: Correct. The standard is a person with 'normal' tolerance and self-restraint. This deliberately excludes D's personal characteristics that merely reduce their capacity for self-control (s.54(3)).

question: R v __________ [2013] EWCA Crim 157 held that voluntary intoxication is irrelevant to the objective test at Stage 3 — D's drunkenness does not lower the standard of tolerance and self-restraint.
feedback: Correct. *R v Asmelash* [2013] — D killed V in a drunken confrontation. The Court of Appeal held that intoxication is not a relevant 'circumstance of D' for the objective test. The standard remains the sober person. S.54(3) excludes circumstances that merely bear on D's capacity for tolerance and self-restraint.

question: R v __________ [2017] EWCA Crim 2061 held that mental disorders that merely reduce D's capacity for tolerance and self-restraint are excluded from the 'circumstances of D' at Stage 3, though they may be relevant to the gravity of the trigger at Stage 2.
feedback: Correct. *R v Rejmanski* [2017] — D, a former soldier with PTSD and adjustment disorder. The court distinguished between factors relevant to the gravity of the trigger (Stage 2 — all of D's circumstances are relevant) and factors relevant to D's capacity for self-restraint (Stage 3 — excluded under s.54(3)). Mental disorders reducing restraint are excluded from the objective standard.

question: Place the analysis of the objective test (Stage 3) under s.54(1)(c) in the correct order:
answer: Identify D's sex and age → Set the standard: a person of normal tolerance and self-restraint → Consider the 'circumstances of D' (excluding factors that merely reduce tolerance) → Ask: might such a person have acted the same or similarly? → Conclude on Stage 3

options.text: Identify D's sex and age (the only personal characteristics expressly included)
Set the standard: a person of normal tolerance and self-restraint (s.54(1)(c))
Consider the 'circumstances of D' — excluding anything bearing only on tolerance/self-restraint (s.54(3))
Ask: might a person with those characteristics have reacted in the same or a similar way?
Conclude: if yes, Stage 3 satisfied; if no, the defence fails

feedback: Correct. This is the structured approach to the objective test. The exclusion under s.54(3) is critical — intoxication, mental disorders affecting restraint, and temperament are not relevant at this stage. Sex and age are expressly included, as are the external circumstances of the qualifying trigger.
oscoaReferences: Coroners and Justice Act 2009, s.54(1)(c), s.54(3)

question: Dwayne, a 25-year-old man with a history of anger management problems and a diagnosed intermittent explosive disorder, kills a neighbour who has been playing loud music for months. Dwayne raises loss of control. At Stage 3, applying *Rejmanski* [2017] and s.54(3):

options.text: Dwayne's intermittent explosive disorder is a relevant 'circumstance of D' and the objective standard must accommodate his reduced self-control
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Rejmanski* [2017] — mental disorders that merely reduce capacity for tolerance and self-restraint are excluded from Stage 3 by s.54(3).

options.text: Dwayne's disorder, which reduces his tolerance and self-restraint, is excluded from the objective test under s.54(3) — the standard remains a person of his sex and age with normal tolerance and self-restraint
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Rejmanski* [2017] and s.54(3), circumstances whose only relevance is that they bear on D's general capacity for tolerance and self-restraint are excluded at Stage 3. Dwayne's intermittent explosive disorder falls squarely into this excluded category. The standard is a 25-year-old man with normal tolerance.

options.text: Dwayne's disorder is entirely irrelevant at every stage of the defence
options.feedback: Incorrect. The disorder is excluded at Stage 3 but may be relevant to the gravity of the qualifying trigger at Stage 2 — for example, whether the things done/said had an extremely grave character from D's perspective.

options.text: Dwayne's disorder and the noise complaint together satisfy the objective test because a person with such a condition might have killed
options.feedback: Incorrect. This directly contradicts s.54(3). The objective test does not lower the bar for D's reduced self-control — it asks whether a person of normal tolerance would have done the same thing.

feedback: The distinction between Stage 2 and Stage 3 is crucial. At Stage 2, all of D's circumstances (including mental disorders) are relevant to assessing the gravity of the trigger. At Stage 3, s.54(3) and *Rejmanski* [2017] exclude circumstances that merely affect D's general capacity for tolerance and self-restraint. The court recognised that such Ds may instead rely on diminished responsibility.

question:
Spot the legal error in this argument:

'My client was drunk at the time of the killing. Under the objective test at Stage 3, the jury must consider what a drunk person of his sex and age with normal tolerance would have done. Alcohol intoxication is a 'circumstance of D' that lowers the standard of self-control expected.'
answer: Intoxication is NOT relevant to the objective test. *R v Asmelash* [2013] — voluntary intoxication does not lower the standard. The jury considers a sober person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance. S.54(3) excludes intoxication because its only relevance is to D's reduced capacity for tolerance and self-restraint.
feedback: Found it. *R v Asmelash* [2013] EWCA Crim 157 is direct authority that voluntary intoxication cannot be a 'circumstance of D' for the objective test. The standard is a sober person with normal tolerance. If D was voluntarily drunk, that is D's own responsibility — it does not dilute the normative standard the law demands.

question: Under s.54(1)(c) and s.54(3) CJA 2009, which TWO of the following are correctly included or excluded from the 'circumstances of D' at Stage 3?

options.text: D's short temper and irritability are included — the jury must consider whether a short-tempered person would have reacted the same way
options.feedback: Incorrect. Characteristics that merely affect D's general capacity for tolerance and self-restraint are excluded under s.54(3).

options.text: D's sex and age are expressly included as relevant factors under s.54(1)(c)
options.feedback: Correct. Section 54(1)(c) expressly includes D's sex and age in the comparator. A 16-year-old girl may be judged against a different standard from a 40-year-old man.

options.text: D's voluntary intoxication is a relevant circumstance that lowers the standard of self-control expected
options.feedback: Incorrect. *R v Asmelash* [2013] — voluntary intoxication is excluded. The standard remains that of a sober person.

options.text: Mental disorders that merely reduce D's capacity for tolerance and self-restraint are excluded from the objective test under s.54(3)
options.feedback: Correct. *R v Rejmanski* [2017] — to the extent a mental disorder merely diminishes self-restraint, it is excluded from Stage 3. However, it may be relevant at Stage 2 in assessing the gravity of the trigger.

options.text: D's cultural background is always excluded from the 'circumstances of D'
options.feedback: Incorrect. Cultural background may be relevant as a circumstance of D, particularly where it affects the gravity of the qualifying trigger. It is not automatically excluded unless its only relevance is to D's capacity for tolerance.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). Sex and age are expressly included. Mental disorders reducing self-restraint are excluded at Stage 3 (*Rejmanski* [2017]) but relevant at Stage 2. Intoxication is excluded (*Asmelash* [2013]). The distinction between what 'bears on gravity of the trigger' (Stage 2) and what 'bears on capacity for self-restraint' (Stage 3) is the key analytical tool.
oscoaReferences: Coroners and Justice Act 2009, s.54(1)(c), s.54(3)

question: Sasha, a 17-year-old girl, has been trafficked into sex work and repeatedly raped by her exploiter, Viktor. Viktor tells her, 'You're worthless — no one will ever love you. Tonight I'm bringing more men.' Sasha, in a state of overwhelming fear and distress, kills Viktor with a knife. At Stage 3, applying s.54(1)(c) and *Rejmanski* [2017]:

options.text: The objective test fails — Sasha's trauma and PTSD are excluded under s.54(3) and the standard is a person with normal tolerance who would never kill
options.feedback: Incorrect. While mental conditions reducing self-restraint are excluded at Stage 3, the circumstances of the qualifying trigger — the trafficking, repeated rapes, and imminent threat of further sexual violence — are part of the 'circumstances of D' at Stage 2 and may justify a finding at Stage 3.

options.text: The objective test is irrelevant — Sasha was a child and the defence is automatically available
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no automatic availability for children. Sasha's age is relevant (s.54(1)(c) expressly includes age) but the full three-stage test must still be satisfied.

options.text: Sasha's response cannot satisfy the objective test because she used disproportionate force — a knife against an unarmed man
options.feedback: Incorrect. The objective test at Stage 3 asks whether a person of D's sex and age with normal tolerance 'might have reacted in the same or a similar way.' It is a comparative question, not a self-defence proportionality assessment.

options.text: The jury may find that a 17-year-old girl of normal tolerance, in Sasha's circumstances (trafficking, repeated rape, imminent further assault), might have reacted similarly — the gravity of the trigger is profound, and Sasha's age is expressly included in the comparator
options.feedback: Correct. Under s.54(1)(c), the objective comparator is a person of Sasha's sex and age with normal tolerance. The circumstances — trafficking, repeated rape, and imminent threat — are relevant to the gravity of the trigger and context. A jury may find that even a person of normal tolerance, in those extreme circumstances, might have lost self-control and killed.

feedback: This scenario brings together multiple elements of the objective test: D's age is expressly included; the circumstances of the qualifying trigger (profound victimisation) feed into the gravity assessment at Stage 2; and the question is whether a person of that description *might* (not would) have acted similarly. The test recognises that even normative standards shift in exceptionally grave circumstances.
oscoaReferences: Coroners and Justice Act 2009, s.54(1)(c), s.54(3)

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] In 2020, Peter discovers his wife has had an affair. When she admits it and tells him she is leaving, Peter loses control and kills her. At trial, Peter seeks to rely on sexual infidelity as the qualifying trigger for loss of control. Applying s.55(6)(c) and *Clinton* [2012]:

options.text: The defence succeeds — the old law allowed sexual infidelity as a trigger, and Peter's wife's admission was the sole cause of his loss of control
options.feedback: Incorrect. The killing occurred in 2020, so the CJA 2009 applies. Sexual infidelity is expressly excluded as a standalone trigger under s.55(6)(c).

options.text: The defence fails automatically — sexual infidelity is wholly irrelevant and must be entirely excluded from consideration
options.feedback: Incorrect. *Clinton* [2012] held that sexual infidelity, while excluded as a standalone trigger, may form part of the contextual background. It is not erased entirely.

options.text: Sexual infidelity alone cannot constitute a qualifying trigger, but if the wife's words when admitting the affair were of an 'extremely grave character' and caused a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged, they may independently constitute a trigger under s.55(4)
options.feedback: Correct. This is the *Clinton* [2012] distinction. The sexual infidelity is excluded (s.55(6)(c)) but Peter's wife's words — her admission, announcement, and any related taunts — are separately assessable under s.55(4). If those words meet the extremely-grave-character threshold, a qualifying trigger exists. The sexual context provides relevant background.

options.text: Peter can rely on the fear trigger because he feared the emotional violence of being abandoned
options.feedback: Incorrect. The fear trigger under s.55(3) requires fear of serious physical violence, not emotional harm or abandonment.

feedback: This consolidates Block A: the interaction between s.55(6)(c) and *Clinton* [2012]. The exclusion of sexual infidelity is real but not absolute. Where infidelity is intertwined with words or conduct of an extremely grave character, the latter may independently qualify. The key is to separate the sexual conduct from the surrounding words/conduct.
oscoaReferences: Coroners and Justice Act 2009, s.55(6)(c), s.55(4)

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] The case that established that sexual infidelity, while excluded as a standalone qualifying trigger, may form part of the contextual background was R v __________ [2012] EWCA Crim 2.
feedback: Correct. *R v Clinton* [2012] — the Court of Appeal held that s.55(6)(c) does not require the total erasure of sexual infidelity from the narrative. It may contextually inform other triggers.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks B & C] A 19-year-old woman, Ella, has been violently abused by her partner for three years. One night, he says, 'I'm going to kill you tonight,' and moves towards her. Ella, terrified and exhausted, grabs a knife and stabs him dead. Evaluate the loss of control defence applying all three stages:

options.text: The defence fails because Ella used a weapon — loss of control is not available to defendants who arm themselves
options.feedback: Incorrect. There is no categorical exclusion for weapon use. It goes to the objective test (Stage 3), not to the availability of the defence.

options.text: Stage 1 (loss of self-control) likely satisfied; Stage 2 — the fear trigger applies (genuine fear of serious violence based on the threat and abuse history); Stage 3 — a 19-year-old woman of normal tolerance in Ella's circumstances might have acted similarly
options.feedback: Correct. The three stages are all engaged. Stage 1: Ella's fear and exhaustion indicate loss of control. Stage 2: The history of abuse and the immediate death threat constitute the fear trigger (s.55(3)). Stage 3: A 19-year-old woman of normal tolerance, facing a genuine threat of death from a long-term abuser, might well act similarly. Sasha's age is expressly included (s.54(1)(c)).

options.text: The defence fails at Stage 3 because a person of normal tolerance would never kill, even in fear
options.feedback: Incorrect. The test at Stage 3 is whether a person of the same sex and age with normal tolerance 'might' have done the same — not whether they would definitively have done so. The threshold is real but not absolute.

options.text: Ella is only entitled to the partial defence of diminished responsibility, not loss of control
options.feedback: Incorrect. Loss of control and diminished responsibility are distinct partial defences. Ella may satisfy both, but loss of control is independently available where the three stages are met.

feedback: This consolidates the full three-stage framework. Domestic abuse cases engage all three stages: loss of self-control (which may be 'slow-burn'), the fear trigger (reinforced by a history of violence), and the objective test (where D's sex, age, and the extreme circumstances of abuse are relevant).

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Which TWO of the following correctly state principles of the loss of control defence under the CJA 2009?

options.text: The loss of self-control need not be sudden — s.54(2) abolished the old common law requirement of suddenness
options.feedback: Correct. Section 54(2) CJA 2009 expressly provides that the loss of self-control need not be sudden. This permits 'slow-burn' reactions, particularly in domestic abuse cases.

options.text: Voluntary intoxication is a relevant 'circumstance of D' that lowers the objective standard of self-control
options.feedback: Incorrect. *R v Asmelash* [2013] — voluntary intoxication is excluded under s.54(3) as a mere factor reducing tolerance and self-restraint.

options.text: The fear trigger and the anger trigger are mutually exclusive — D cannot rely on both
options.feedback: Incorrect. Section 55(5) expressly allows the triggers to operate in combination. Many cases involve both fear and a sense of being seriously wronged.

options.text: The objective test at Stage 3 excludes circumstances whose only relevance is that they bear on D's general capacity for tolerance and self-restraint
options.feedback: Correct. Section 54(3) — this is the critical exclusion. Mental disorders reducing restraint, short temper, and intoxication are all excluded under this provision (*Rejmanski* [2017]; *Asmelash* [2013]).

options.text: The old law of provocation under s.3 Homicide Act 1957 continues to apply alongside the new CJA 2009 provisions
options.feedback: Incorrect. The old law was replaced, not supplemented. The CJA 2009 repealed s.3 of the Homicide Act 1957. The new loss of control defence applies exclusively to killings on or after 4 October 2010.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (d). The CJA 2009 abolished the suddenness requirement and introduced s.54(3) to exclude factors merely bearing on personal capacity for self-control. These two features represent some of the most significant reforms to the partial defence. The triggers may combine, and the old law has been fully repealed.

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] Under s.55(4) CJA 2009, the things done or said must constitute circumstances of an extremely grave character AND must have caused D to have a __________ sense of being seriously wronged.
feedback: Correct. The s.55(4) trigger requires a 'justifiable' sense of being seriously wronged. This is an objective test — the sense of grievance must be one that would be recognised as reasonable, not merely D's subjective feeling. *Bowyer* [2013] — a burglar has no justifiable sense of grievance.
